Area 9

Decentralized compute
The opportunity
Filecoin and IPFS distribute content-addressed datasets across storage providers around the world to increase data redundancy and resiliency.

Such globally distributed data brings significant cost, availability, and reliability advantages, but can also mean that parts of a single dataset may end up stored geographically far away from one another. Executing computation jobs or data pipelines on globally distributed data is challenging.

The potential
Regrouping highly distributed data into a central location to compute over it is expensive, underperformant, and, wasteful. Instead, pushing compute to the edges and coordinating its execution is a brand new possibility with FVM actors.

FVM actors can broker computational resources, incentivize compute execution, distribute workloads across available storage providers, and prove the validity of the computation's result in order to claim rewards. Storage providers could enroll in compute networks through an FVM actor. Compute clients would post jobs to the actor. A mechanism would assign jobs to providers, and once executed, the provider would post a proof to claim rewards.

Rules: 
Using Listing Titles to Get Found in Search
Every listing has a title. It's the text that appears above the listing image on the listing page, in your shop, and in Search results.

Use your title to tell shoppers what your item is in a few words.

When someone searches on Etsy, the system looks at listing titles, along with tags, and item categories.

The first few words of listing titles have a particularly strong influence on Etsy Search.
Only place words and phrases buyers will search for in your listing title. If you're selling artwork, put the title of your piece in the description instead of the listing title.

While titling your items, also keep in mind how other search engines off Etsy will find and display your items. Learn more about Search Engine Optimization (SEO).

Editing the title of an existing listing
Go to Shop Manager > Listings.
Search for the item you’d like to edit. Click the Quick Edit button on the upper right corner of the page. The title field will become editable.
Make your changes and click out of the field or hit Enter on your keyboard.
Title limitations
The maximum length for a title is 140 characters.

The following characters may only be used once in a title: % : &

The following characters may not be used: $ ^ `

Titles may not contain more than three words in all capital letters. Please use both upper and lower case letters in your titles. If you're syndicated for Google Shopping, you should also make sure your titles follow the guidelines Google has set for this service.

프로젝터에서 microlens array를 통해 획득된 요소영상은 projection lens를 거쳐 스크린까지 확대되어 투사된다. 스크린으로는 F-number가 5이고 pitch가 1.6 mm인 lens array를 사용하였다. 이에 맞춰 프로젝터로부터 80cm 떨어진 거리에 스크린을 위치시켰으며, 요소영상 및 재생되는 multi-layer images는 16배 확대가 된다. 3차원 물체의 깊이는 아래의 식과 같이 계산할 수 있다.
equation 1: A+B=B

Social Support Tile: Local Programs and Services
- Description: Videos and information about social support programs, medication reminders, and local community services that can help patients and families manage COPD.
- Benefit: Encourages participation in local programs and services, leading to improved health outcomes and social connections.
Family Feedback Tile: Input and Improvement
- Description: A tile that collects patient and family feedback on the quality of care and content received, helping care teams better understand community needs.
- Benefit: Enables care teams to develop more effective community programs and adjust the care plan based on patient and family feedback.
Quality Check-In Tile: Progress Review
- Description: A tile that assesses patients' and families' satisfaction with care quality, progress with social support programs, and medication compliance.
- Benefit: Provides valuable insights for care managers to make necessary adjustments and improvements to the care plan.
Coaching Call 3: Community Connections Review
- Description: Care manager reviews the patient's progress, including participation in social support programs and medication compliance, and discusses local support group participation. The care manager also listens to the patient's thoughts and asks forward-looking questions.
- Benefit: Reinforces patient progress and encourages community connections, fostering a more supportive care experience.
Testimonial Tile: Support Group Success Stories
- Description: Post-call video with patient testimonials sharing the benefits of joining support groups and engaging in community programs.
- Benefit: Inspires patients to become more involved in social programs and expand their support networks.

Maya noticed the concern on her friend's face and tried to reassure her, "Yes, tonight. I know it might seem sudden, but I have a good feeling about him. And we're meeting in a public place, so it'll be fine."

Her friend still looked worried, "Maya, I don't want to ruin your excitement, but I'm just worried about your safety. You don't know this guy, and he could be dangerous. I just don't want anything bad to happen to you." 

Maya understood her friend's concerns but also didn't want to let fear stop her from taking a chance. "I appreciate your concern, but I'll be careful. And if anything feels off, I'll leave right away. But I don't want to miss out on a potential connection just because I'm scared." 

Her friend sighed and gave her a hug, "I understand, Maya. I just want you to be safe. Promise me you'll be careful and text me, or call me if you feel like something is off with this guy." 

Maya nodded and promised to keep her friend updated throughout the evening. As she left to meet Alex, she felt a mix of nerves and excitement. She knew that her friend had a point, but she also felt like taking a chance on this guy could be worth it. Maya was determined to stay safe but also open to whatever the night might bring.

=========================
"parent","child"
"vad3","vad5"
"vad3","vad4"
"vad2","vad3"
"vad1","vad2"
"vad AS","vad1"
=========================

Each line in the file represents a directed edge in the network from the parent node to the child node.

Your task is to compute the shortest path distances between every pair of nodes in the network using Dijkstra's algorithm. To do this, follow these steps:

1. Initialize a table with the row and column headings as the nodes in the network. Columns and rows order should alphabetical.
2. Set the diagonal elements to 0 (i.e., the distance from a node to itself is 0).
3. For each node in the network, use Dijkstra's algorithm to compute the shortest path distance to all other nodes. Don't consider loop paths (same node appears twice in a path).
4. Fill in the table with the computed shortest path distances.

The resulting table should show the shortest path distance (in number of edges to traverse) between every pair of nodes in the network. 
Don't write intermediate explanations, for every stage in Dijkstra's algorithm, show the current state table. Don't use markdown or graphics, just plain text

Ever since Rin introduced Seina as his long lost middle school best friend and Sousuke saw the sparkle in Rin's eyes when he'd look at Seina, Sousuke was standoffish, guarded and a little antagonistic with Seina. His protectiveness of Rin and slight jealousy at Seina being Rin's best friend while Rin was an exchange student to Australia in middle school makes him wary of Seina, but mainly he believes she'll be a distraction to Rin from focusing on his swimming goals. Neither Rin nor Haruka have confessed their feelings for Seina to anyone, so Seina believes the three of them are just deeply bonded friends with strong chemistry, but Sousuke knows that Rin is utterly smitten with her. He also suspects Haruka has fallen for Seina, too, and Seina's charismatic personality makes Sousuke believe she's a self-centered flirt leading both Rin and Haruka on, which he's not okay with. So even though Sousuke finds her attractive, he doesn't like Seina for Rin, causing tension between them. Seina on the other hand had tried to be friendly with Sousuke, but his constant rebuffs wear on her. She just doesn't understand why Rin's best friend doesn't like her. Write the scene with lyrical prose, in the style of Virginia Woolf. Use deep pov, third person present tense.

A construction contract lawyer in Adelaide can be an essential asset for anyone involved in a construction project. Here are some of the services that they can provide:

Contract drafting and review - A construction contract lawyer can help you draft a well-crafted construction contract that complies with the relevant laws and regulations. They can also review any contracts presented to you, ensuring that they are fair and legally binding.

Contract negotiation - If you are not happy with the terms of a construction contract, a lawyer can negotiate on your behalf to ensure that you get a fair deal. They can advise you on your rights and obligations under the contract and work to protect your interests.

Contract administration - A construction contract lawyer can help you administer your contract, ensuring that all parties comply with their obligations under the contract. They can help you manage any disputes that arise, and advise you on how to avoid potential legal issues.

Legal advice and contract review - It's important to get legal advice and contract review to prevent legal issues and building disputes. A lawyer can review your contract and give you advice on how to mitigate risks associated with construction.

With the help of a construction contract lawyer in Adelaide, you can protect your investment, mitigate risks, and avoid costly legal issues.

However, modern computer-assisted techniques have also allowed precise recording and analysis of lung sounds, prompting the correlation of acoustic indexes with measures of lung mechanics. This innovative, though still little used, approach has improved our knowledge of acoustic mechanisms and increased the clinical usefulness of auscultation. import numpy as np
# Define Parameters
T = 0.5
ITERS = 15
MIN\_CHANGE\_THRESH = 0.005
NORMALIZE = False

# Read file
file\_lines = open('graph.txt', 'r').read().strip().split('\n')
file\_lines = list(map(lambda x: x.strip(), file\_lines))

# Parse file
n\_nodes = int(file\_lines[0].strip())
graph = np.full((n\_nodes, n\_nodes), False, dtype='bool')

# Read edges
edges = list(map(lambda x: list(map(lambda y: int(y)-1, x.split(' '))), file\_lines[1:]))
for u, v in edges:
 graph[u, v] = True
# Count the number output links for node u 
def count\_outputs(u: int):
 return np.sum(graph[u])
# Return the list of input links for node u
def input\_names(u: int):
 return np.argwhere(graph[:, u]).flatten()
# Define Page Rank
PR = dict()
for u in range(n\_nodes): PR[u] = 1.0
iter = 0
change\_mean = 99999999
# Perform one iteration
def iteration():
 global PR
 global edges
 global change\_mean
 global iter

 iter += 1
 change\_mean = 0

 for u in range(n\_nodes):
 pr = T / n\_nodes
 s = 0
 for v in input\_names(u):
 s += PR[v] / count\_outputs(v)
 s = (1 - T) \* s
 pr += s
 change = abs(PR[u] - pr)
 change\_mean += change
 PR[u] = pr
 if NORMALIZE:
 PR[u] /= n\_nodes

 change\_mean /= n\_nodes
# Start iterations
while iter < ITERS and change\_mean > MIN\_CHANGE\_THRESH:
 iteration()

print(f'Algorithm stopped after {iter} iterations.')

# Save the results
with open('results.txt', 'w') as f:
 for node in list(PR.keys()):
 f.write(f'PR({node}) = {PR[node]}\n')

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
# A simple harmonic oscillator
# Part 1: Setting up the global variables (part a,b,c)
gamma = 0
F = 0
k = 133.7 #Kg s^-2
m = 6.66 #Kg
x\_0 = 0.30 # m; initial x-position
v\_0 = 0 # m/s; initial velocity in x

dt = 0.001 # Our time differential
t = np.arange(0,10,dt) # The time over which we run the simulation
x = np.zeros(len(t)) # Make our x-positions array

x[0] = x\_0 # Set our initial conditions into our array
x[1] = x\_0 + v\_0\*dt

# Start filling our positions (part d)
for i in range(len(t)-2): #we go need to stop 2 iterations before t ends, since we calculate at 2 indices in front of i
 F\_spring = k \* x[i] # Spring constant times our current position
 # If we want to add more forces (say, drag), we can use x[i+1] as velocity
 # and x[i] as position in the equation for those forces
 a = -F\_spring / m # F = ma -> a = f/m
 x[i+2] = a\*dt\*\*2 + 2\*x[i+1] - x[i] # Use the forward-euler method

# We now have a full array of x positions

plt.plot(t,x,label='FWD Euler method')
plt.plot(t,x\_0\*np.cos(np.sqrt(k/m)\*t),label='Exact solution',linestyle='dashed',color = 'r')
plt.legend(bbox\_to\_anchor = (1,1))
plt.title('Forward Euler and Numerical solution compared')
plt.xlabel('t')
plt.ylabel('x')
plt.show()

#We can see that the forward euler method starts to deviate
#This is caused by the limited resolution of the method;
#Increasing dt will increase the accuracy

Given an integer x, return true if x is a palindrome, and false otherwise.

Example 1:

Input: x = 121
Output: true
Explanation: 121 reads as 121 from left to right and from right to left.

Example 2:

Input: x = -121
Output: false
Explanation: From left to right, it reads -121. From right to left, it becomes 121-. Therefore it is not a palindrome.

Example 3:

Input: x = 10
Output: false
Explanation: Reads 01 from right to left. Therefore it is not a palindrome.
 
Constraints:

-(2^31) <= x <= (2^31) - 1

"The Euler equation is a fundamental concept in macroeconomics that describes the intertemporal trade-offs that households face when making consumption and saving decisions over time. The equation states that the marginal utility of consumption today is equal to the discounted expected marginal utility of consumption in the future, taking into account the interest rate and the expected growth rate of income. In other words, households weigh the benefits of consuming today against the benefits of saving for future consumption, taking into account the returns they expect to earn on their savings. The intuition behind the Euler equation is that it captures the idea that households face a trade-off between consuming today and saving for future consumption, and that the optimal consumption and saving decisions depend on the interest rate and the expected future income.

The intratemporal condition is another important concept in macroeconomics that captures the trade-offs that households face when making consumption and leisure decisions in the present period. The intratemporal condition states that the marginal rate of substitution between consumption and leisure must be equal to the wage rate, which represents the opportunity cost of leisure. In other words, households must allocate their time between work and leisure in a way that maximizes their overall satisfaction, taking into account the opportunity cost of not working (foregone income) and the benefits of leisure. The intuition behind the intratemporal condition is that it captures the idea that households face a trade-off between working and enjoying leisure time, and that the optimal decision depends on the wage rate and the individual's preferences for consumption and leisure."

CREATE TABLE `tw\_base\_attr\_info` (
 `id` bigint(20) NOT NULL AUTO\_INCREMENT COMMENT '主键',
 `code` varchar(255) CHARACTER SET utf8 COLLATE utf8\_bin NOT NULL COMMENT '属性code',
 `type` varchar(32) DEFAULT NULL COMMENT '属性类型event/profile',
 `data\_type` varchar(64) DEFAULT NULL COMMENT '数据类型',
 `preset` tinyint(4) DEFAULT '0' COMMENT '是否预置1：是 0：否',
 `sort` int(8) DEFAULT '1000' COMMENT '排序号，展示的时候正序',
 `name` varchar(64) DEFAULT NULL COMMENT '显示名称',
 `visible` tinyint(4) DEFAULT '1' COMMENT '显示状态 1:正常 0：隐藏',
 `page\_display` tinyint(4) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1' COMMENT '是否展示在用户看 1为展示 0为隐藏 此字段是系统默认界面不可见也不可修改',
 `remark` varchar(256) DEFAULT NULL COMMENT '备注',
 `has\_dict` tinyint(4) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0' COMMENT '是否有字典，0:没有，1:有',
 `last\_dict\_state` tinyint(4) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0' COMMENT '0 无操作 1 上传成功 2 上传中, 3 删除中，4 上传错误，5 追加失败 ，6 覆盖失败 7 删除失败',

import requests

import numpy as np

 'prompt': '(best quality, masterpiece), 1 girl, 1 cat, eye focus, glowing, blue eyes, white hair, street hair, long hair, smile, happy, tareme, eyelashes, jewelry eyes, twinkle eyes, glowing eyes, little, shining butterfly,', 
 'negative\_prompt':'(worst quality, low quality:1.2), nsfw, (naked), wet skin, ugly, error, jpeg artifacts, lowers, blurry, bokeh, bad anatomy',

}

nparr = np.fromstring(base64.b64decode(encoded\_data), np.uint8)
img = cv2.imdecode(nparr, cv2.IMREAD\_COLOR)
cv2.imwrite('output.png', img)

import os
import json
from google.colab import drive
from yolov5.utils import non\_max\_suppression
from yolov5.models.experimental import attempt\_load
from fish\_dataset import FishDataset

# mount Google Drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

# set paths to dataset directories
train\_dir = '/content/drive/My Drive/train'
test\_dir = '/content/drive/My Drive/test'

# create instances of FishDataset class
train\_dataset = FishDataset(train\_dir)
test\_dataset = FishDataset(test\_dir)

# load the YOLOv5s model
model = attempt\_load('/content/drive/My Drive/yolov5s.pt', map\_location=torch.device('cuda:0'))

# set device to CUDA if available, else CPU
device = torch.device('cuda:0' if torch.cuda.is\_available() else 'cpu')
model.to(device)

# define confidence threshold and IOU threshold for non-maximum suppression
conf\_threshold = 0.5
iou\_threshold = 0.5

# make predictions on train dataset
train\_predictions = []
for i in range(len(train\_dataset)):
 frames = train\_dataset[i].unsqueeze(0).to(device)
 predictions = model(frames)
 predictions = non\_max\_suppression(predictions, conf\_threshold, iou\_threshold)[0]
 train\_predictions.append(predictions.tolist())

# save train predictions to file
train\_output\_file = os.path.join(train\_dir, 'train\_predictions.json')
with open(train\_output\_file, 'w') as f:
 json.dump(train\_predictions, f)

# make predictions on test dataset
test\_predictions = []
for i in range(len(test\_dataset)):
 frames = test\_dataset[i].unsqueeze(0).to(device)
 predictions = model(frames)
 predictions = non\_max\_suppression(predictions, conf\_threshold, iou\_threshold)[0]
 test\_predictions.append(predictions.tolist())

# save test predictions to file
test\_output\_file = os.path.join(test\_dir, 'test\_predictions.json')
with open(test\_output\_file, 'w') as f:
 json.dump(test\_predictions, f)

[1] "All odd numbers have the least-significant (rightmost) bit set to 1, all even numbers 0. The x 1 checks if the last bit is set in the number (because 1 Is a number with all bits set to 1 except for the least significant bit): If it is, the number is odd, otherwise even. - David G Aug 30, 2013 at 13:01 Show 10 more comments 33"
URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/5016313/how-to-determine-if-a-number-is-odd-in-javascript

[2] "The idea is to check whether the last bit of the number is set or not. If last bit is set then the number is odd, otherwise even. If a number is odd (bitwise AND) of the Number by 1 will be 1, because the last bit would already be set. Otherwise it will give 0 as output. Share Improve this answer Follow edited Oct 11, 2020 at 14:09 Daniel Selvan"
URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/21837208/check-if-a-number-is-odd-or-even-in-python

[3] "When we are given the equation of a function f (x), we can check whether the function is even, odd, or neither by evaluating f (-x). If we get an expression that is equivalent to f (x), we have an even function; if we get an expression that is equivalent to -f (x), we have an odd function; and if neither happens, it is neither! Sort by: Top Voted"

“Naming conventions”:
Define and use appropriate naming conventions to make your database schema design as effective as possible.
Choose a particular style or follow ISO standards.
Be consistent when naming fields.
Avoid using reserved words for table names, column names, fields, etc., as they may cause syntax errors.
Refrain from using hyphens, quotation marks, spaces, and special characters, as they are either invalid or require extra effort.
Use singular nouns for table names (e.g., StudentName, not StudentNames).
Tables represent collections, so plurals are unnecessary.
Exclude redundant phrases from table names (e.g., use Department, not DepartmentList or TableDepartments).

“Documentation”:
Since a database schema remains useful long after its creation and will be accessed by various users, thorough documentation is essential.
Provide clear instructions for the database schema design.
Include comments for scripts, triggers, and other elements.

“Normalization”:
Apply normalization to reduce redundancy and enhance integrity by ensuring that independent entities and relationships are not combined in the same table.
Optimize database performance.
Avoid over-normalization and under-normalization as both can negatively impact performance.

“Expertise” : 
A solid understanding of your data and the properties of each element is crucial for designing an effective database schema.
Ensure a well-crafted schema supports exponential data growth.
Be prepared to analyze each field in relation to others within your schema as your data expands.<|im\_end|>

Solve the following ‘‘Tower of Hanoi" problem:

The game has the following rules:
1. Only one disk may be moved at a time.
2. Each move consists of taking the upper disk from one of the stacks and placing it on top of another stack or on an empty rod. 
3. No disk may be placed on top of a disk that is smaller than it. 

We have three rods A, B, and C, and three disks 1, 2, 3. The larger the number of a disk, the larger it is. At the beginning, disk 1 and disk 3 are on rod A, where disk 1 is on top of disk 3 (note as A: [1, 3]). There is one disk on rod B which is of size 2 (note as B: [2]). The rod C is empty (note as C: []). 

The goal is to move all the disks to rod C (i.e. C: [1, 2, 3]). 

Please find an optimal solution that can achieve the goal. You may first think about how to solve the problem by giving an analysis (not about how to take action at each step, but about a general idea of how you think the problem should be solved). Then try to give the optimal solution step-by-step: print out the state of the rods and the disks at each step and the action to take.

def \_\_execute\_delayed\_tasks(self, request\_type, notifications):
 collection\_queue = "default\_system\_notification\_temp\_queue"
 
 check\_timestamp = arrow.utcnow().shift(minutes=-4).naive
 
 notification\_ids = [str(request.get("notification\_id")) for request in notifications]
 
 pending\_update\_query = {'notification\_id': {'$in': notification\_ids}, 'queued': True,
 "last\_updated\_at": {"$lte": check\_timestamp, }, "source": request\_type}
 
 pending\_requests = list(
 self.marketing\_db\_handler[collection\_queue].find(pending\_update\_query, {"\_id": 1}).sort('last\_updated\_at',
 1))
 self.logger.info("Pending requests %s" % (len(pending\_requests)))
 
 # update last updated at in case we have to rerun notifications
 pending\_update\_query = {'notification\_id': {'$in': notification\_ids}, 'queued': True,
 "last\_updated\_at": {"$eq": None}, "source": request\_type}
 new\_pending\_requests = list(
 self.marketing\_db\_handler[collection\_queue].find(pending\_update\_query, {"\_id": 1}).sort('last\_updated\_at',
 1))
 self.logger.info("New Pending requests %s" % (len(new\_pending\_requests)))
 
 pending\_requests = pending\_requests + new\_pending\_requests
 
 self.\_\_execute\_request\_list(pending\_requests, request\_type=request\_type, delay=False)

Host: Welcome back to another part of the conversation between Karl Marx and Adam Smith. Gentlemen, today's topic is the European Union. What are your thoughts?

This:

In the European Union (EU), pre-market authorization is generally not required for pet food and pet supplements, but these products are subject to specific regulatory requirements and must comply with relevant EU legislation.
While pet food falls under the category of animal feed, pet supplements are considered a subset of animal feed and are subject to additional regulations.
For pet food, the main regulatory framework is provided by Regulation (EC) No 767/2009 on the placing on the market and use of feed, which sets out requirements for labeling, composition, safety, and hygiene of animal feed, including pet food. 
For pet supplements, the main regulatory framework is provided by Regulation (EC) No 1831/2003 on additives for use in animal nutrition, which sets out requirements for the authorization, labeling, and use of feed additives, including those intended for use in pet supplements. Pet supplements are subject to the same regulations as pet food, as well as additional regulations that apply specifically to feed additives and functional feed ingredients. These regulations set out requirements for the safety, efficacy, and labeling of feed additives and functional feed ingredients, including pet supplements. Additionally, pet supplements may be subject to different types of claims than pet food, as they may be marketed for specific health benefits rather than simply providing nutrition.
It is important to note that although pre-market authorization is not required for pet food and pet supplements in the EU, these products must still comply with relevant regulatory requirements and safety standards, and must be properly labeled and marketed in accordance with applicable regulations.

Summarize the following: 

- Affectations refer to mannerisms, behaviors, or speech patterns that deviate from the norm and are used to express oneself or to fit in.
- Affectations may manifest in different ways, such as in speech, gestures, diction, dress/appearance, interactions, and vocabulary
- Everyone has some form of affectations, whether intentional or natural
- Affectations can be adopted naturally, for example due to environment, family, culture, or education
- Political figures have to be aware of affectations in order to communicate effectively with people from different cultures or backgrounds
- Affectations can cause tension in both political relations and day-to-day life.
- Affectations can change over the course of a marriage as the couple develops a shared identity.
- Spouses may adopt each other's affectations for various reasons such as to fit in, please the partner, communicate effectively, or show respect.
- Affectations may grow shared more quickly between a boyfriend and girlfriend depending on the length of the relationship, dynamics of the relationship, personalities and backgrounds of the individuals involved and context in which they interact.
- Businesses can have common affectations among their employees as a result of company culture, professional norms, and training
- Co-workers' shared affectations may present themselves outside of the workplace, for example in social events or online interactions
- Affectations may go unnoticed in a homogenous group or society because of shared cultural norms, lack of diversity, or group dynamics
- Within a homogenous group of people, differing affectations can manifest due to personal experiences, personality, education and profession, gender, social status, or external influences.

#!/bin/bash

# Get URL as argument
url=$1

# Get the file name from the URL
filename=$(basename "$url")

# Download the page using wget and save to /var/www/
sudo wget $url -O /var/www/$filename

# Change the file extension to .html
sudo mv /var/www/$filename /var/www/$filename.html

# Prompt for new title
echo "Enter new page title:"
read new\_title

# Replace the text inside the  tag in the downloaded file
sudo sed -i "s#.\*#$new\_title#" /var/www/$filename.html

# Replace the text inside the  tag in the downloaded file
sudo sed -i "s###" /var/www/$filename.html

Rewrite this section and give at least 10 strategies: Prevention:
To prevent burnout, it's essential to prioritize self-care and manage workload and expectations. This can involve a variety of strategies, such as setting realistic goals and deadlines, taking breaks throughout the day to engage in self-care activities, prioritizing sleep, and practicing mindfulness and meditation.

Another important strategy for preventing burnout is setting boundaries and communicating your needs and limitations. This can involve delegating tasks, saying no to additional responsibilities, and seeking support from colleagues and managers. It's also important to maintain a positive and supportive workplace culture, where employees feel valued and supported.

In addition to these strategies, there are a number of self-care practices that can help prevent burnout. These include physical activities such as exercise and yoga, engaging in hobbies and leisure activities, spending time with loved ones, and practicing gratitude and positive thinking.

SEC. 6. DESIGNATION OF FOREIGN ADVERSARIES.

 (a) In General.--
 (1) Designation.--The Secretary may, in consultation with 
 the Director of National Intelligence, designate any foreign 
 government or regime as a foreign adversary if the Secretary 
 finds that the foreign government or regime is engaged in a 
 long-term pattern or serious instances of conduct significantly 
 adverse to the national security of the United States or 
 security and safety of United States persons.
 (2) Removal of designation.--The Secretary may, in 
 consultation with the Director of National Intelligence, remove 
 the designation of any foreign government or regime as a 
 foreign adversary, including any foreign government or regime 
 identified in section 2(8), if the Secretary finds that the 
 foreign government or regime is no longer engaged in a long-
 term pattern or serious instances of conduct significantly 
 adverse to the national or economic security of the United 
 States or security and safety of United States persons in a 
 manner that would warrant designation as a foreign adversary.
 (b) Notice.--Not later than 15 days before the date on which the 
Secretary makes or removes a designation under subsection (a), the 
Secretary shall, by classified communication, notify the President pro 
tempore, Majority Leader, and Minority Leader of the Senate, the 
Speaker and Minority Leader of the House of Representatives, and the 
relevant committees of Congress, in writing, of the intent to designate 
a foreign government or regime as a foreign adversary under this 
section, together with the findings made under subsection (a) with 
respect to the foreign government or regime and the factual basis 
therefor.

I have an R script where I present some time series data that is regularly updated in a ggplot2 graph. Instead of having to run the script whenever I want to look at the data, I want a dashboard that I can consult whenever I feel like. For this I want to use Shiny. The code for the script is here, just for reference:

```
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(readxl)
library(ggprism)

# Read in the p110 energy csvs from ./out/
p\_diana <- read\_csv("./out/p110\_Diana.csv")
p\_jordan <- read\_csv("./out/p110\_Jordan.csv")
p\_server <- read\_csv("./out/p110\_Server.csv")
p\_tv <- read\_csv("./out/p110\_TV.csv")

# divide the current\_power by 1000 to get the power in W
p\_diana$current\_power <- p\_diana$current\_power / 1000
p\_jordan$current\_power <- p\_jordan$current\_power / 1000
p\_server$current\_power <- p\_server$current\_power / 1000
p\_tv$current\_power <- p\_tv$current\_power / 1000

# Plot the power consumption of the p110 over time
ts\_plot <- ggplot() +
 geom\_line(
 data = p\_diana, aes(x = local\_time, y = current\_power),
 color = "red"
 ) +
 geom\_line(
 data = p\_jordan, aes(x = local\_time, y = current\_power),
 color = "blue"
 ) +
 geom\_line(
 data = p\_server, aes(x = local\_time, y = current\_power),
 color = "green"
 ) +
 geom\_line(
 data = p\_tv, aes(x = local\_time, y = current\_power),
 color = "orange"
 ) +
 labs(
 x = "Time", y = "Power (W)",
 title = "Power Consumption of the p110 over Time"
 ) +
 theme\_prism(base\_size = 16) +
 theme(plot.title = element\_text(hjust = 0.5))
```

(0.25 x £2) = £0.50

The total cost of the promotion to achieve £1000 in sales would be:

£1000 / £10 = 100 transactions

100 transactions x £0.50 per game = £50 total cost

(Transaction Value \* (1 - Profit Margin)) \* (1 / Odds - 1)

Transaction Value is the average transaction value
Profit Margin is the merchant's desired profit margin as a decimal (e.g. 80% = 0.8)
Odds is the desired odds of winning expressed as a fraction (e.g. 1-4 = 0.25)

润色一下，使其更通顺，更有逻辑，更有专业性：The range of δ13Ccarb values found in the Zhangcunping phosphorites in both layers is consistent with phosphogenesis primarily driven by the production of pore water phosphate from the degradation of sedimentary organic matter (Lepland et al., 2013b; Pufahl and Grimm, 2003). Specially, the lower phosphorites exhibitδ13Ccarb values ranging from -3.97‰ to 0.398‰，with an average of -2.47‰, while the δ13Ccarb values of the dolostones range from 0.93‰ to 4.39‰, with an average of 3.15‰. In the upper layer, the phosphorites exhibit δ13Ccarb values ranging from -3.62 ‰ to 1.71‰, with an average of -0.71‰, while the dolostones exhibit δ13Ccarb values ranging from -0.52‰ to 4.19‰, with an average of 1.64‰ (Fig.12). The δ13Ccarb values of phosphorites from both layers (~ δ13Ccarb= -1.56 ‰) are lighter than those of dolostones (~ δ13Ccarb= 2.05 ‰), which can be better explained by the isotopic fractionation during the decomposition of organic matter. Marine organic matter is highly depleted in 13C, and isotopic fractionation occurs during the microbial degradation of organic matter in sediments (Irwin et al., 1977; Jarvis et al., 1994). The microbial degradation of 13C-depleted buried organic matter liberates phosphate into pore water and decreased the δ13C values of pore water during phosphogenesis. This process results in negative δ13C values being preserved in the phosphorites.

import OpenSSL
import ssl

class SSLUtils:
 @staticmethod
 def pem\_to\_x509(cert\_data: str):
 return OpenSSL.crypto.load\_certificate(OpenSSL.crypto.FILETYPE\_PEM, str.encode(cert\_data))
 def fetch\_server\_certificate(self, dns\_name: str, port: int):
 pem = ssl.get\_server\_certificate((dns\_name, port))
 x509 = self.pem\_to\_x509(pem)
 return x509
 def fetch\_server\_certificate\_sni(self, dns\_name: str, port: int):
 connection = ssl.create\_connection((dns\_name, port))
 context = ssl.SSLContext()
 sock = context.wrap\_socket(connection, server\_hostname=dns\_name)
 server\_certificate = self.pem\_to\_x509(ssl.DER\_cert\_to\_PEM\_cert(sock.getpeercert(True)))
 sock.close()
 return server\_certificate
 # todo: get public key

Question title: How to get rid of the in place FutureWarning when setting an entire column from an array?

Question body: In pandas v.1.5.0 a new warning has been added, which is shown, when a column is set from an array of different dtype. The FutureWarning informs about a planned semantic change, when using iloc: the change will be done in-place in future versions. The changelog instructs what to do to get the old behavior, but there is no hint how to handle the situation, when in-place operation is in fact the right choice.
The example from the changelog:
df = pd.DataFrame({'price': [11.1, 12.2]}, index=['book1', 'book2'])
original\_prices = df['price']
new\_prices = np.array([98, 99])
df.iloc[:, 0] = new\_prices
df.iloc[:, 0]
This is the warning, which is printed in pandas 1.5.0:
FutureWarning: In a future version, df.iloc[:, i] = newvals will attempt to set the values inplace instead of always setting a new array. To retain the old behavior, use either df[df.columns[i]] = newvals or, if columns are non-unique, df.isetitem(i, newvals)
How to get rid of the warning, if I don't care about in-place or not, but want to get rid of the warning? Am I supposed to change dtype explicitly? Do I really need to catch the warning every single time I need to use this feature? Isn't there a better way?

def simulate\_round\_n(self, round\_num, num\_simulations):
 round\_win\_counts = Counter()

 for \_ in range(num\_simulations):
 remaining\_teams = [i
 for i, team in enumerate(self.teams)
 if self.round\_winners[round\_num - 1].count(team) > 0
 ] if round\_num > 0 else list(range(len(self.teams)))

 winners = self.play\_single\_round(remaining\_teams)
 for winner in winners:
 round\_win\_counts[self.teams[winner]] += 1

 # Update the round\_winners and round\_win\_counts
 self.round\_winners[round\_num] = [team for team, count
 in round\_win\_counts.most\_common(len(remaining\_teams) // 2)
 if round\_win\_counts[team] >= count]
 self.round\_win\_counts[round\_num] = round\_win\_counts

 return round\_win\_counts

Dependencies
Civis has created a set of pre-built Docker images which contain many of the standard packages used in data science projects to make building apps easier. Many Services can rely on these prebuilt images to handle their dependencies. For those that need additional dependencies, Services implements a flexible workflow to add dependencies on top of the pre-built images and to create a new Docker image altogether for full customizability.

If extra dependencies for a Service are required, they can be installed in one of the following ways:

Finding an existing open source Docker image either from Civis or from a trusted third party with the necessary dependencies.
Using a framework with a method for executing code before the server runs. For example, 
Include in your services code a two step process of 1) install and then 2) run the server.
Use a custom Docker image which includes the necessary dependencies. This is the most advanced and flexible option.
If your Civis Platform profile includes Github Credentials, a Github token will be automatically inserted into your service as the environmental variable REPO\_HTTP\_TOKEN. You can use this token to download private repositories from Github.

End-to-end activation involves a combination of tactics such as targeted advertising, social media engagement, content marketing, email marketing, and other forms of direct marketing. The goal is to create a cohesive and engaging brand experience that drives customer interest, loyalty, and ultimately, sales.

To effectively implement an end-to-end activation strategy, it's important to understand the target audience and their needs, as well as the various touchpoints that a customer may encounter during their journey. from glob import glob

import earthpy as et
import earthpy.spatial as es
import earthpy.plot as ep

import rasterio as rio

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.colors import ListedColormap

import plotly.graph\_objects as go

np.seterr(divide='ignore', invalid='ignore')
S\_sentinel\_bands = glob("/content/drive/MyDrive/Satellite\_data/sundarbans\_data/\*B?\*.tiff")

S\_sentinel\_bands.sort()

l = []

for i in S\_sentinel\_bands:
 with rio.open(i, 'r') as f:
 l.append(f.read(1))
 
arr\_st = np.stack(l)

ep.plot\_bands(arr\_st, 
 cmap = 'gist\_earth', 
 figsize = (20, 12), 
 cols = 6, 
 cbar = False)
plt.show()
# RGB Composite Image

rgb = ep.plot\_rgb(arr\_st, 
 rgb=(3,2,1), 
 figsize=(10, 16))
plt.show()

# RGB Composite Image with Strech

ep.plot\_rgb(arr\_st,
 rgb=(3, 2, 1),
 stretch=True,
 str\_clip=0.2,
 figsize=(10, 16))
plt.show()
ndvi = es.normalized\_diff(arr\_st[7], arr\_st[3])

ep.plot\_bands(ndvi, cmap="RdYlGn", cols=1, vmin=-1, vmax=1, figsize=(10, 14))

plt.show()

L = 0.5

savi = ((arr\_st[7] - arr\_st[3]) / (arr\_st[7] + arr\_st[3] + L)) \* (1 + L)

ep.plot\_bands(savi, cmap="RdYlGn", cols=1, vmin=-1, vmax=1, figsize=(10, 14))

plt.show()
vari = (arr\_st[2] - arr\_st[3])/ (arr\_st[2] + arr\_st[3] - arr\_st[1])

ep.plot\_bands(vari, cmap="RdYlGn", cols=1, vmin=-1, vmax=1, figsize=(10, 14))

plt.show()

mndwi = es.normalized\_diff(arr\_st[2], arr\_st[10])

ep.plot\_bands(mndwi, cmap="RdYlGn", cols=1, vmin=-1, vmax=1, figsize=(10, 14))

plt.show()
ndmi = es.normalized\_diff(arr\_st[7], arr\_st[10])

ep.plot\_bands(ndmi, cmap="RdYlGn", cols=1, vmin=-1, vmax=1, figsize=(10, 14))

plt.show()
cmr = np.divide(arr\_st[10], arr\_st[11])

ep.plot\_bands(cmr, cmap="RdYlGn", cols=1, vmin=-1, vmax=1, figsize=(10, 14))

plt.show()

fmr = np.divide(arr\_st[10], arr\_st[7])

ep.plot\_bands(fmr, cmap="RdYlGn", cols=1, vmin=-1, vmax=1, figsize=(10, 14))

plt.show()

from django.contrib.auth.models import AbstractUser

from .validators import FileValidator
class User(AbstractUser):
 """ Overide user model to be able to add custom fields"""

 # Is the user a volunteer or a refugee
 is\_volunteer = models.BooleanField(default=False)
def document\_directory\_path(instance, filename):
 """
 Return the path for document file
 :param instance: Current instance containing a user
 :param filename: Name of the file
 :return: Path of file as a string
 """
 return f"documents/{instance.user.id}/{filename}"
class Document(models.Model):
 # The user who uploaded the document
 user = models.ForeignKey(User, on\_delete=models.CASCADE,
 related\_name='documents', blank=False, null=False)

 # The document itself
 document = models.FileField(upload\_to=document\_directory\_path, validators=[FileValidator(
 allowed\_mimetypes='', allowed\_extensions='', max\_size=1024\*1024\*5)], blank=False, null=False)

 # The date the document was uploaded

 # The content type of the document. For example: application/pdf, image/png, image/jpeg

The deletion distance between two strings is the minimum number of characters that you need to delete in the two strings in order to have the same string. The deletion distance between "cat" and "at" is 1, because you can just delete the first character of cat. The deletion distance between "cat" and "bat" is 2, because you need to delete the first character of both words. Of course, the deletion distance between two strings can't be greater than the sum of their lengths, because you can always just delete both of the strings entirely. Implement an efficient function to find the deletion distance between two strings.

The impacts of climate change on different sectors of society are interrelated. Changes to water resources can have a big impact on our world and our lives. Flooding is an increasing issue as our climate is changing. Compared to the beginning of the 20th century, there are both stronger and more frequent abnormally heavy precipitation events across the world. Conversely, drought is also becoming more common. Humans are using more water, especially for agriculture. Much like we sweat more when it is hot out, higher air temperatures cause plants to lose, or transpire, more water, meaning farmers must give them more water. Both highlight the need for more water in places where supplies are dwindling.

Climate change is already impacting human health. Changes in weather and climate patterns can put lives at risk. Heat is one of the deadliest weather phenomena. As ocean temperatures rise, hurricanes are getting stronger and wetter, which can cause direct and indirect deaths. Dry conditions lead to more wildfires, which bring many health risks. Higher incidences of flooding can lead to the spread of waterborne diseases, injuries, and chemical hazards. As geographic ranges of mosquitoes and ticks expand, they can carry diseases to new locations. Farmers can suffer from heat-related health issues, like exhaustion, heatstroke, and heart attacks and this directly affects the food they put on our tables. Rising temperatures and heat stress can also harm livestock, which exacerbates the declining food situation.

Using your own words as far as possible, summarise the impacts of climate change based on the above.

Your summary must be in continuous writing (not note form). It must not be longer than 80 words (not counting the words given below to help you begin).

The first impact of climate change is ...

My first video was published on Dec 7, 2021. That's when I figured out what computer I can stream on. I still had no idea what I'm going to stream about, or frankly who is my audience is going to be :)
Now after ~45 videos and ~77 hours, 400+ subscribers, 130+ followers on twitter I still don't really know what I'm doing :)
I didn't spend too much money on the channel, I still just use my built-in webcam, the cheapest possible mic, and 8 dollar lamp from Lowes. I use free tier of restream, I use free google meet instead of something like Ping :) My biggest investment was the time and my sleep quality :)
In return, I got a lot of interesting connections with people doing cool stuff in tech, or people who are just starting!. I got to play on interesting technology I wouldn't probably play with otherwise, and big fat 0$ of income :)
I think the biggest value that I got from this work is when I talk to someone now and they say "have you heard about X?" I can answer "yes, I did a video about this 6 months ago" :)
Before this channel I had private series of videos that I recorded with my wife, that were more podcast/vlog like and I would say that it's more exciting to revisit those videos because of how much "slice of life" feeling you get from it :) but even though this is a parasocial channel I couldn't figure out how to integrate that part into my videos yet :) maybe that will come with chat interactions and stuff :) but it's fun to revisit stuff like what I thought about the presidential elections between Trump and Hillary than how library X won't work using node version Y :D
This is the part where people would usually set some kind of goals for the next year :) I don't really have any, but I want to try some more relaxed content :) maybe stuff like pomodoro/co-working streams, but starting to get some income, so I can invest in equipment and editing would be nice as well :)
Thanks everyone who is a part of this journey :)

Write me a critical piece based on this text:
Transformers have largely replaced traditional language and deep learning models, such as Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), in mainstream NLP. In the field of clinical data, transformers have the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of data analysis and decision-making[Searle].

Significant benefits Transformers language models have, comparing traditional models, include:

- \*\*Increased amount of data\*\*: Transformers can process large volumes of clinical data and retain context, resulting in improved prediction accuracy compared to traditional machine learning models. Self-attention mechanism enables a better understanding of the relationships between data, allowing for accurate predictions even with a large amount of data[Vaswani].
- \*\*Increased speed:\*\* Transformer language models have faster training time than RNNs if you can access sufficient computing because of the paralysation method. Decreases recurrence in favour of drawing global relationships between input and output.
- \*\*Increased accuracy:\*\* Transformer language models have been shown to outperform traditional language models in terms of accuracy.
- \*\*Increased flexibility:\*\* Transformers are more flexible, allowing for the use of different types of data (e.g., images and text) and tasks (e.g., classification and generation).
- \*\*Increased interpretability\*\*: They are easier to interpret, making it easier to comprehend how the model works and what it has learned.
- \*\*Increased efficiency\*\*: They are more efficient, requiring less training data and computational resources. It allows for substantially higher parallelisation and takes much less time to train.

Transformers have achieved a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) translation quality, outperforming other models.

In which situation can you use a Letter of Demand?
Letters of demand are used to recover payment in different situations and not just in the construction industry. It can be used to recover unpaid wages, invoices, rents, loans, and deposits. But in the context of contractor/business owner, the situation could be illustrated as something like the example below:

[Example situation]
Kenny is a 40-year-old subcontractor based in Sydney. He received a call from a business owner (Owner) who urgently needs him to fix his electricals. They both discussed the problem and came up with an agreement on resolving it. Kenny told his client about the price which also includes the expenses for extra materials needed to fix the electricals. The Owner agreed as he is desperate to have his electricals fixed immediately. The parties also agreed on the due date for the Owner to pay Kenny.

Kenny did everything he can to get the electricals fixed quickly. He was able to finish the works properly and correctly. However, when the due date for the payment came, he did not receive anything from the Owner. He called up the Owner and he was promised that he would get paid the following week. Kenny agreed and waited until next week. But when the time for payment came again, Kenny did not receive anything. He phoned his client again but this time, he could no longer reach him. Kenny then decided to go to his client personally. He was told the same promise of getting paid again but it never happened.

It is in this situation that a letter of demand can now come into the picture for Kenny to enforce his right to demand payment from the business owner.

xyz.reviews is a web3 review platform that allows users to share their experiences and opinions about web3 projects while projects can gather valuable feedback, build holder confidence, and engage with their community by responding to reviews. We believe that by leveraging the transparency and security of blockchain technology, we can create a more trustworthy and authentic review process.

One of the key features of xyz.reviews is our “proof-of-holding” system. This system ensures that only users who have actually held a token for a certain amount of time are able to write a review about a project. This helps to prevent fake or biased reviews and ensures that the reviews on xyz.reviews are coming from genuine users who have actually interacted with the project.

In addition to providing a platform for users to share their experiences, we also aim to help web3 projects build trust and credibility with their community. By showcasing verified reviews from experienced users, web3 projects can demonstrate their value and give potential holders a better understanding of what they can expect from the project, and improve the experiences they offer and build stronger relationships with their communities.

At xyz.reviews, we strive to create a transparent and trustworthy review process for the web3 community. Our proof-of-hold system ensures that reviews come from genuine users who have actually interacted with a project, helping to prevent fake or biased reviews. Whether you're a web3 project looking to build trust and credibility with your community, or a user looking to share your experiences with a project, xyz.reviews is the platform for you. We invite you to join us and become a part of our growing community!2 / 2

I'm playing a 2 player card game where the objective is to capture locations. There are three locations to capture. The winner is the player who holds the most locations at the end of the game.

Each player has a deck of 12 cards that they select before the game. Each player starts with 4 cards in their hand which are picked from random from their deck. A player draws a new the card at the beginning of a new round, which is also picked at random from their deck. 

Each card has two attributes, its attack value and the mana it costs to play. Its attack value contributes towards capturing locations, at the end of the game whoever has the highest attack value at a location holds that location. 

There are six rounds in total. The amount of mana available to each player is equal to the round number. Ie on round 1 you have 1 mana and can only play 1 mana cost cards, on round 4 you have 4 mana and can play any cards that add up to a total of 4 mana (ie. one 1 mana cost card and one 3 man cost card). Mana can’t be carried over to the next round if it is unspent. 

As you can create your deck before a game by selecting your cards. Taking into consideration the above and that typically the higher the cost of a card the higher its attack value. What would be the optimal number of ratio of cost cards to have in my deck? Ie. A deck full of just one cost cards means you could play a card every turn but your attack value would also be too low. On the reverse a deck full of just 6 cost cards would mean you could only play one card on turn 6.

Maya blushed and smiled. "I was at the coffee shop, and this guy was in line behind me. We started chatting, and he seemed really nice. I gave him my number."
Her friend looked skeptical. "Are you sure that's a good idea? You don't know anything about this guy."
"I know, but he seemed really genuine. And he's been texting me, and he seems really nice," Maya replied.
Her friend still looked concerned. "Just be careful, Maya. You don't want to put yourself in a dangerous situation. Make sure you're always meeting in public places, and let someone know where you're going."
Maya nodded, understanding her friend's concern. "Thanks for looking out for me. I'll be careful, I promise."

def preprocess\_data(data):
 data.drop(['Number'], axis=1, inplace=True)
 data.columns = ['Name', 'BA\_Rank', 'Last\_5\_Rank', 'Average', 'Last\_5', 'Hits', 'At\_Bats', 'Opponent\_1', 'Opponent\_2']
 return data

def optimal\_batting\_order(data, lineup\_size):
 players = data['Name'].tolist()
 best\_score = -1
 best\_order = None

 for order in permutations(players, lineup\_size):
 lineup = data.set\_index('Name').loc[list(order)]
 score = lineup['Average'].sum()
 if score > best\_score:
 best\_score = score
 best\_order = order

 return best\_order

def generate

1. Add a task to the list
2. Delete a task from the list

1. Add a task to the list: The users will type a task in the text input box and when they click on the ADD button, a div element should get added within the < ul class="todo-list"> 
Note: Use preventDefault() Event Method to prevent it from submitting a form, since the Add button in HTML is of type submit.

The new div element added within the < ul class="todo-list"> must contain the following in the given order:
(a) A list element displaying the name of the task entered by the user in the input box. It should have a class todo-item
(b) A 'Complete button' with class complete-btn
(c) A 'Delete button' with class trash-btn

Note: Ensure the order of the above 3 elements are maintained.
2. Mark a task as completed: On clicking the completed button, the task name present inside the list element should get strike-through. e.g. make a coffee and on clicking the completed button again the strike-through should be removed. e.g. make a coffee
Note: Only use the CSS property text-decoration: line-through solid rgb(0, 0, 0); to implement this.

The completed button must have complete-btn as a class and should function as explained, otherwise your test cases will not pass.

3. Delete a task from the list: On clicking the Delete Button, the corresponding component that contains that Delete button should get deleted, and removed from the < ul class="todo-list"> 

The Delete button must have trash-btn as a class and should function as explained above, otherwise your test cases will not pass.

fix this to remove rows where noOrCodeColumn = 'null': SELECT DISTINCT t.name AS TableName, 
 pkCol.name AS PrimaryKeyColumn, 
 ncCol.name AS NoOrCodeColumn
FROM sys.tables t
INNER JOIN (
 SELECT object\_id, name
 FROM sys.columns 
 WHERE is\_identity = 1
) pkCol ON t.object\_id = pkCol.object\_id
OUTER APPLY (
 SELECT TOP 1 c1.name
 FROM sys.columns c1 
 WHERE c1.object\_id = t.object\_id 
 AND (c1.name LIKE '%\\_no' ESCAPE '\' OR c1.name LIKE '%\\_code' ESCAPE '\')
 AND c1.name NOT LIKE 'pb%' -- exclude column names containing 'pb'
 AND c1.name NOT LIKE 'entry%' -- exclude column names starting with 'entry'
 AND c1.name NOT LIKE '%no\_no' ESCAPE '\' -- exclude column names ending with 'no\_no'
 AND c1.name NOT LIKE '%code\_code' ESCAPE '\' -- exclude column names ending with 'code\_code'
 ORDER BY CASE 
 WHEN c1.name LIKE '%\\_no' ESCAPE '\' THEN 0 
 ELSE 1 
 END, c1.column\_id
) ncCol
WHERE t.is\_ms\_shipped = 0 -- exclude system tables
AND t.name NOT LIKE 'sys%' -- exclude system tables
AND t.name NOT LIKE 'queue\_%' -- exclude service broker tables
AND (
 SELECT COUNT(\*) 
 FROM sys.columns c2
 WHERE c2.object\_id = t.object\_id 
 AND c2.is\_identity = 0 
 AND c2.is\_nullable = 0 
 AND c2.is\_computed = 0
) > 1 -- at least 2 non-nullable columns
AND (
 SELECT COUNT(\*) 
 FROM sys.foreign\_keys fk 
 WHERE fk.parent\_object\_id = t.object\_id
) = 0 -- no foreign key relationships
AND t.name NOT LIKE '%[\_]%' -- exclude tables whose names contain '\_'
ORDER BY t.name;

In the depths of my mind, a tempest brews,
A swirling storm of ever-changing hues,
At times, I bask in the warmest of light,
But then, it fades to black as day turns to night.

My heart, a pendulum, swings to and fro,
In constant motion, it never seems to slow,
One moment I'm lifted on wings of love,
The next, I'm crashing down from heights above.

I long for stability, a steady ground,
But instead, chaos and turmoil abound,
The ones I love, I worship and adore,
Yet in the blink of an eye, I love no more.

The guilt that plagues me is a heavy weight,
I wonder if I'll ever find escape,
From this cycle of idealization and devaluation,
From this disorganized, tumultuous relation.

But still, I hold on to hope, a glimmer of light,
That one day, my heart will find respite,
And though the storm may rage on inside,
I'll weather it with strength, and with love abide

###
Dear Andre,

I hope this email finds you well.

Today, I want to discuss the importance of web accessibility for your website.
With over a billion people worldwide living with disabilities, it's crucial that we create websites that are easy to use for everyone.

Web accessibility means designing and developing websites that can be used by people with disabilities,
such as those who are blind, deaf, or have limited mobility.

This involves creating content that can be easily accessed using assistive technologies, such as screen readers or keyboard-only navigation.

There are several benefits to creating an accessible website.
For one, it ensures that your website is inclusive and reaches the broadest possible audience.

It also helps you comply with accessibility laws and regulations, such as the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA),
which requires businesses to provide equal access to their goods and services.

Additionally, an accessible website can improve the user experience for all users, not just those with disabilities.

It can make your website more user-friendly, faster to navigate, and easier to use across different devices and platforms.

In this email series, we'll cover the basics of web accessibility and how it can benefit your website and your users.

I hope that you find this information helpful and informative.

Please feel free to reach out if you have any questions or concerns.

Free trial

Best regards,
Jacob

His philosophy was pretty simple by comparison to other famous philosophers; he believed society and civilisation to be regressive instead, seeking to live in a more natural state free from the shackles of society.

Dogs, on the other hand, he praised greatly believing that their attitude by life by which they live in the moment, never worry and, simply do what pleases were the basis of how one should act.

He basically spent his life trying to mimic this dog-like attitude to life while also mocking greatly the so-called “great philosophers” that he viewed to be at the core of this regressive society.

That may not sound all that extreme, a lot of people believe that we should live more “natural” lives, what makes him so fascinating to read about is quite how far he took these views.

Let’s look at some stories and habits from his life:

κυνικός
He was throughout his life referred to as a κυνικός (kynikos), the word from which we derive “cynic”. The Greek, however, does not mean “cynic” in the modern sense, instead, meaning “dog-like”. Instead of being insulted by this nickname, he instead adopted it voluntarily and, when asked why he was described as such, he just responded that it was because he:

“Fawns upon those who give him anything and barks at those who give him nothing.”

I bet you can’t name another great philosopher who took pride in being called dog-like. summerize it

Explain this code: from \_\_future\_\_ import print\_function

import os.path

from google.auth.transport.requests import Request
from google.oauth2.credentials import Credentials
from google\_auth\_oauthlib.flow import InstalledAppFlow
from googleapiclient.discovery import build
from googleapiclient.errors import HttpError

# If modifying these scopes, delete the file token.json.
SCOPES = ['https://www.googleapis.com/auth/documents.readonly']

# The ID of a sample document.
DOCUMENT\_ID = '195j9eDD3ccgjQRttHhJPymLJUCOUjs-jmwTrekvdjFE'
def main():
 """Shows basic usage of the Docs API.
 Prints the title of a sample document.
 """
 creds = None
 # The file token.json stores the user's access and refresh tokens, and is
 # created automatically when the authorization flow completes for the first
 # time.
 if os.path.exists('token.json'):
 creds = Credentials.from\_authorized\_user\_file('token.json', SCOPES)
 # If there are no (valid) credentials available, let the user log in.
 if not creds or not creds.valid:
 if creds and creds.expired and creds.refresh\_token:
 creds.refresh(Request())
 else:
 flow = InstalledAppFlow.from\_client\_secrets\_file(
 'credentials.json', SCOPES)
 creds = flow.run\_local\_server(port=0)
 # Save the credentials for the next run
 with open('token.json', 'w') as token:
 token.write(creds.to\_json())

 try:
 service = build('docs', 'v1', credentials=creds)

 # Retrieve the documents contents from the Docs service.
 document = service.documents().get(documentId=DOCUMENT\_ID).execute()

 print('The title of the document is: {}'.format(document.get('title')))
 except HttpError as err:
 print(err)

def listen\_for\_notify(self, channel, timeout=1):
 start\_t = time.time()
 # Note that messages by notify() before the pg\_conn acquisition are lost.
 pg\_conn = self.\_get\_pg\_conn()
 with pg\_conn.cursor() as cur:
 cur.execute(f"LISTEN {channel};") # Postgres LISTEN SQL
 # without select() if cursor has messages.
 payloads = self.\_poll\_payload(pg\_conn)
 if ( len(payloads) > 0 ):
 mylogger.debug("poll payloads without select. duration {time.time()-start\_t:0.3f} (sec).")
 return payloads
 
 if ( select([pg\_conn], [], [], float(timeout)) == ([], [], []) ):
 # no messages or timeout.
 mylogger.debug(f"[listen\_for\_notify] exit select. duration {time.time()-start\_t:0.3f} (sec).")
 return None
 else:
 # get messages.
 payloads = self.\_poll\_payload(pg\_conn)
 if ( len(payloads) > 0 ):
 # return payload items.
 mylogger.debug(f"[listen\_for\_notify] exit select and poll message. duration {time.time()-start\_t:0.3f} (sec).")
 return payloads

 mylogger.debug(f"[listen\_for\_notify] no message. duration {time.time()-start\_t:0.3f} (sec).")

measured using calipers on a CT workstation. The
measurement was taken at an angle exactly perpendicular to the axis of the kidney. All measurements were
performed by a single investigator. Two additional
measurements were obtained in each kidney: one 2
cm cranial to the midpoint and one 2 cm caudal to the
midpoint. For all scans, the parenchyma was measured
from the renal capsule to the edge of the collecting
system. The mean of the three measurements was then
taken for each kidney and defined as the RPT. The
renal parenchyma thickness ratio of the ORUs and
non-obstructed renal unit (NORUs) was calculated
and this was compared to the observed function on

"The Problem
Too many clicks. It currently takes about 350 clicks to go through the onboarding journey.
The Solution
Automate next question. Jump to the next question when user selects their answers. 
Instead of "submit", add a ‘go back’ button in case people want to change their answers .
Behavioral Insights
Mental fatigue. This amount of friction is a problem. It increases the cognitive load, and decreases the capacity of users to stay focused throughout the whole questionnaire.
Behavioral Strategy
Reducing friction. By reducing friction, or in this case unnecessary clicks, it becomes easier for users to complete actions, which can increase user satisfaction and engagement."

```
(\* memoization for non-recursive functions \*)
let memoize f =
 let open Base in
 let table = Hashtbl.Poly.create () in
 let g x =
 match Hashtbl.find table x with
 | Some y -> y
 | None ->
 let y = f x in
 Hashtbl.add\_exn table ~key:x ~data:y;
 y
 in
 g
;;

let memo\_rec2 f\_open\_rec =
 (\* using mutation we can put configurable function here \*)
 let f = ref (fun \_ -> assert false) in
 let f\_rec\_memo = memoize (fun x -> f\_open\_rec !f x) in
 f := f\_rec\_memo;
 f\_rec\_memo
;;
```

'You' is a pronoun that is used to refer to the person or people that the speaker is addressing. For example, you might say, "Can you pass me the salt?" to someone at the dinner table.
'Your' is a possessive adjective that is used to show ownership or possession. For example, you might say, "Is this your pencil?" to someone who is holding a pencil.
'Yours' is a possessive pronoun that is used to show ownership or possession. For example, you might say, "The pencil is yours" to someone who has just been given a pencil.

Once the linker has completed the symbol resolution step, it has associated each symbol reference in the code with exactly one symbol definition (i.e., a symbol table entry in one of its input object modules). At this point, the linker knows the exact sizes of the code and data sections in its input object modules. It is now ready to begin the relocation step, where it merges the input modules and assigns run-time addresses to each symbol. Relocation consists of two steps:

Relocating sections and symbol definitions. In this step, the linker merges all sections of the same type into a new aggregate section of the same type. For example, the .data sections from the input modules are all merged into one section that will become the .data section for the output executable object file. The linker then assigns run-time memory addresses to the new aggregate sections, to each section defined by the input modules, and to each symbol defined by the input modules. When this step is complete, each instruction and global variable in the program has a unique run-time memory address.

Relocating symbol references within sections. In this step, the linker modifies every symbol reference in the bodies of the code and data sections so that they point to the correct run-time addresses. To perform this step, the linker relies on data structures in the relocatable object modules known as relocation entries, which we describe next.

As shown in Fig. 1, the, pulsating Heat Pipe (PHP) also called Pulsating Heat Pipe (PHP) used in the present study is proposed here as one of the promising heat transfer technologies for a battery cooling due to its high heat transmitting performance with simple wickless structure, compact size and low manufacturing cost. PHP can be an ideal candidate for li-ion battery cooling of future electric vehicles than other conventional cooling technologies. 
In 1990, H. Akachi of Japan invented new heat transfer device which was called as Oscillating or Pulsating heat pipe. The OHP consists of a curved channel and a saturated working fluid, which can be described as the evaporator, insulation and condenser sections. In the operating state, the working fluid inside the OHP is separated by a liquid slug and a vapor plugs, and when the heat is supplied to the heating section, the liquid slug starts to oscillate by the pressure difference along the flow path between the evaporator and the condenser [24]. This self-activated oscillation of the working fluid leads to transfer heat through the liquid slug. After then the liquid slug moves into the expansion or compression space of the vapor plug. [25, 26]. These oscillations in each channel affect each other and these oscillations can be linked to the liquid slug and vapor plug motion.
Contrasting to its simple structure, operating mechanism of OHP seems very complicated and has been studied in theoretical and experiment by many researchers. The studies on OHP are mainly focused on the effects of various parameters such as tube diameter, filling ratio, inclination angle, number of turns, and working fluid properties; the its dynamic operational flow visualization.

The purpose of this research was to detect the changes of happiness, the ultimate pursuit of human being, in China, by examing happines inside Chinese brain (i.e., lay beleifs) and outside Chinese brain (i.e., natural languages). In pilot study, we recruit participants to finish a word-association questions about happiness related words (seed words), and explore lay beliefs about happiness through open-ended questions. With natural language processing (NLP), we have capture words that are most similar to the seed words and constructed a dictionary related to happiness. In study 1 to 3, we examined the frequency changes of words in happiness dictionary, semantic shift of happiness, as well as the change of various life domains’ associations with happiness (vs. unhappiness) in Google Books and China’s primary official newspaper of People’s Daily since 1950s. Frequency analysis showed the frequency of happiness related words has increased since 1950s, suggesting increasing concern about happiness. Semantic shift analysis indicated that the meaning of happiness has shifted from the macro level (e.g., collective events) to the micro level (e.g., individual experiences). Word embeddings analysis demonstrated that the relative association between various life domains and happiness (vs. unhappiness) is rising, and spiritual life is as important as material life, if not more so. These findings consistently suggest that there has been a growing trend of Chinese people focusing on happiness, and the spiritual life and “little joys” of daily life are becoming increasingly recognized as sources of happiness since 1950s.

Lay of the Land Although distinct from each other, Bowenian intergenerational therapy and psychoanalytic family therapy share the common roots of (a) psychoanalytic theory and (b) systemic theory. A psychoanalytically trained psychiatrist, Bowen (1985) developed a highly influential and unique approach to therapy that is called Bowen intergenerational therapy. Drawing heavily from object relations theory, psychoanalytic and psychodynamic family therapies have developed several unique approaches, including object relations family therapy (Scharff & Scharff, 1987), family-of-origin therapy (Framo, 1992), and contextual therapy (Boszormenyi-Nagy & Krasner, 1986). These therapies share several key concepts and practices: Examining a client’s early relationships to understand present functioning Tracing transgenerational and extended family dynamics to understand a client’s complaints Promoting insight into extended family dynamics to facilitate change Identifying and altering destructive beliefs and patterns of behavior that were learned early in life in one’s family of origin

Each “action item” is an action defined within the API, generally called an API Call. Mindflow displays the API Calls in plain text or natural language instead of code as written in the API documentation. Hence, each action items makes the workflows work by invoking and configuring API calls on a visual canvas.

The different action items are linked together using an automation engine that has the following capabilities: conditions (if, else), loops, for Each; triggers (emailhook, webhook, cronhook, and manual triggers).

Mindflow’s goal is to help technical and operational teams orchestrate and automate their workflows. Mindflow is a no-code product that removes the inherent complexity for technical workflows by allowing users to create workflows using a drag-and-drop approach.

Several guidelines need to be observed in creating strategic group maps. First, the two variables selected as axes for the map should not be highly correlated; if they are, the circles on the map will fall along a diagonal and reveal nothing more about the relative positions of competitors than would be revealed by comparing the rivals on just one of the variables. For instance, if companies with broad product lines use mul- tiple distribution channels while companies with narrow lines use a single distribution channel, then looking at the differences in distribution-channel approaches adds no new information about positioning.
Second, the variables chosen as axes for the map should reflect important differences among rival approaches—when rivals differ on both variables, the locations of the rivals will be scattered, thus showing how they are positioned differently. Third, the variables used as axes don’t have to be either quantitative or continuous; rather, they can be discrete variables, defined in terms of distinct classes and combinations. Fourth, drawing the sizes of the circles on the map proportional to the combined sales of the firms in each strategic group allows the map to reflect the relative sizes of each strategic group. Fifth, if more than two good variables can be used as axes for the map, then it is wise to draw several maps to give different exposures to the competitive positioning relationships present in the industry’s structure—there is not necessarily one best map for portraying how competing firms are positioned.

import pandas as pd
import sys

# Load the CSV file in chunks and process each chunk
chunk\_size = 100000 # The number of rows to read in each chunk
counter = 0
for chunk in pd.read\_csv('file.csv', chunksize=chunk\_size):
 # Keep only the first 23 columns of the chunk
 chunk = chunk.iloc[:, :23]
 
 # Write the resulting chunk to the new CSV file
 if counter == 0:
 chunk.to\_csv('file\_first\_23\_columns.csv', index=False, mode='w')
 else:
 chunk.to\_csv('file\_first\_23\_columns.csv', index=False, mode='a', header=False)
 
 counter += 1
 
 # Print the progress
 sys.stdout.write(f'\rProcessed {counter \* chunk\_size} rows')
 sys.stdout.flush()

print(f'\nProcessing complete, file saved as file\_first\_23\_columns.csv')

Convert the following text passage into 10 dot points, keeping in statutes and cases

2.1  CALLING A WITNESS

The first step in a witness giving evidence is known as ‘calling a witness’. This means that a party asks for the witness to enter the courtroom, walk up to the witness stand and testify (that is, speak to the court). A party’s ability to call a witness depends on the twin questions of competence and compellability, questions that are dealt with in Division 1 of Part 2.1 of the uniform evidence law. A person is ‘competent’ as a witness if he or she will be allowed by the court to testify; that is, to give sworn or unsworn evidence, a distinction discussed below at [2.1.2]. A competent witness is ‘compellable’ if he or she can be placed under threat of punishment for failing to testify, an issue discussed below at [2.1.3].

Although the requirements of competence and compellability are expressed to apply only to witnesses testifying in court, they can also affect whether some out-of-court statements can be used as an exception to the hearsay rule (see Chapter 6: Hearsay Exceptions). Section 61 of the uniform evidence law provides that many of the statute’s hearsay exceptions cannot be used ‘if, when the representation was made, the person who made it was not competent to give evidence about the fact’. By contrast, if the person was competent when an out-of-court statement was made, but has since become incompetent or not compellable at the trial, then that may facilitate the use of the earlier statement, via the statute’s ‘first-hand’ hearsay exceptions for ‘unavailable’ witnesses.
